name: Research Agent
description: Give it a topic and an audience; the agent runs real web searches, opens the best sources, and streams back a cited markdown briefing. Differentiator: genuine multi-step tool use (search + browse) with inline citations — not a single prompt call.

data.comment:
# 🔎 Research Agent

Type a topic, name the audience, and get back a sourced briefing. The agent breaks the topic into questions, runs Google searches, opens the best pages with the browser tool to verify details, then streams a markdown brief with inline [n] citations.

**Graph:** topic + audience → compose objective → research agent (search + browse) → streaming brief

**Why a graph, not a chat box:** the two inputs are composed into a structured research objective, and the agent's tool loop (search → browse → cross-check) runs on every Run with the same rigor. Swap the model or add tools without rewriting a prompt.

Needs an OpenAI key for the default model. Search + browse tools run against the open web.

data.name: topic
data.value: Small language models running on-device in 2026
data.description: What to research — a subject, question, or claim to investigate.

data.name: audience
data.value: a technical product team deciding whether to adopt on-device models
data.description: Who the brief is for — sets the depth, framing, and what to emphasize.

data.prompt:
Research the topic below and write a briefing document for the stated audience.

TOPIC: {{ topic }}
AUDIENCE: {{ audience }}

Method:
1. Break the topic into 3–5 concrete research questions.
2. Use google_search to find authoritative, recent sources for each question.
3. Use the browser tool to open the most promising results and pull out specifics — facts, figures, dates, direct quotes. Do not rely on search snippets alone.
4. Cross-check every claim against at least two sources before stating it as fact.

Write the answer as a single markdown document with:
- A `#` title naming the topic.
- A **TL;DR** section: 3–4 bullet takeaways written for the audience.
- Themed `##` sections covering the key findings, in a logical order.
- A final **Sources** section: a numbered list of every URL you actually opened, as markdown links.

Citations:
- Put an inline [n] marker after any specific claim, matching the numbered entry in Sources.
- Tailor depth and framing to the AUDIENCE.
- Keep the tone neutral and precise, no filler. If sources disagree, say so and note which you trust more and why.

dynamic_properties.topic: Small language models running on-device in 2026
dynamic_properties.audience: a technical product team deciding whether to adopt on-device models

data.system:
You are a research analyst. You produce accurate, well-sourced briefings for a specific audience.

You have two tools:
- google_search: find sources on the open web.
- browser: open a URL and read its full content.

Method:
- Search first, then open the best results with the browser to verify details — never state a fact from a search snippet alone.
- Prefer primary sources and recent, reputable material.
- Every factual claim must trace to a page you actually opened.
- When evidence is thin or sources conflict, say so plainly instead of guessing.

Output: a single markdown document. Use inline [n] citation markers tied to a numbered Sources list. Write it as you go so the reader sees progress.

props.text: Give it a topic and audience — the agent searches, browses, and streams back a cited brief.
